title: Absorption In The Visual Field
definition: A state where the higher-order parts of the visual cortex stop constructing the world of 'things', leaving a more primitive experience of light, color, and undifferentiated form.

difficulty_levels.beginner: When you look at something for too long, like tiles or a wall, your brain can start to blur them together into a sea of colors. This is called absorption in the visual field and it's a way to give your brain a rest.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: Absorption in the visual field is a neurological state where the constant object-identification machinery takes a break, allowing a more primitive experience of light, color, and undifferentiated form. It's related to phenomena like perceptual rivalry, sensory adaptation, and the Troxler fading effect.
difficulty_levels.expert: Absorption in the visual field is a point where two things converge: The physical 'tuning out', as the sensory adaptation in the visual cortex causes the blurring of static input, and the conscious 'letting go', as the higher-order parts of the visual cortex halt the usual cognitive framing. This state allows for an experience of pure consciousness, without content, which is a goal of many contemplative traditions.

extra_fields.insight: Absorption in the visual field is not just a random mental glitch; it is a direct, intuitive taste of one of the core goals of many meditation practices.
